body:
{
  "title": "Finley Okonkwo — Product Designer, Anchor Codec",
  "compiled_truth": "Finley Okonkwo is a product designer at [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor), where they've been shaping the developer experience layer since joining in early 2027. Before Anchor, Finley spent several years designing for developer-facing tools — API consoles, CLI workflows, onboarding sequences — at a mid-stage infrastructure company that was eventually acqui-hired. That experience left them with a deep, somewhat unusual understanding of how enterprise buyers actually evaluate tooling: not through feature matrices, but through the first five minutes of interaction.

Finley joined [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) at a moment when the company was transitioning from pure technical validation to broader enterprise adoption. The timing mattered. Anchor's single-pass primitives were powerful but the initial developer experience was, by most accounts, rough. Documentation was sparse, the dashboard felt like an afterthought, and the onboarding path assumed a level of familiarity with deterministic data infrastructure that most new users simply didn't have. Okonkwo's first major project was redesigning the entire onboarding flow — from account creation through first successful integration — and the results were measurable. Enterprise trial-to-paid conversion jumped noticeably in Q2 2027, and several customer success leads credited the new flow directly.

Internally, Finley is known for two things: obsessive attention to product detail and a willingness to push back on engineering timelines if it means shipping something that actually feels right. They've described their design philosophy as "remove the seam" — the idea that a well-designed developer tool should never force the user to consciously switch contexts or mental models. Colleagues sometimes joke that Finley treats every pixel with the seriousness of a load-bearing wall.

They tend to work closely with the developer relations and documentation teams, and have been increasingly involved in shaping Anchor's public-facing API reference. There's a running internal debate about wether Finley's role has effectively expanded into product management territory, but Okonkwo has resisted the formal title shift, preferring to stay hands-on with design artifacts. They prototype prolifically — often shipping interactive mockups before specs are even finalized — and this habit has become a core part of how Anchor's product team iterates.

Outside of work, Finley occasionally speaks at small design meetups, usually on the topic of designing for technical audiences. They're quiet on social media but have a modest following among developer-tools designers who share their work in niche communities.",
  "timeline": "- **2027-02-01** | Finley Okonkwo joins [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) as Product Designer, initially focused on developer onboarding.
- **2027-03-14** | Kicks off a full audit of Anchor's existing onboarding flow; interviews 12 enterprise trial users over two weeks.
- **2027-05-02** | New onboarding experience ships to all accounts. Internal metrics show a significant increase in trial-to-paid conversion within the first month.
- **2027-06-19** | Presents "Designing for Deterministic Infrastructure" at a small developer tools design meetup in Brooklyn.
- **2027-08-30** | Begins collaborating with the docs team on a redesigned API reference, introducing interactive code samples directly in the documentation.
- **2027-10-11** | Leads a cross-functional sprint to overhaul the Anchor dashboard's integration status view, addressing a top-3 enterprise support request.
- **2028-01-15** | Promoted informally to lead product designer; still declines a shift to product management despite internal discussions.
- **2028-03-22** | Ships a prototype for Anchor's new multi-tenant workspace UI, which enters beta with three enterprise customers.
- **2028-05-07** | Runs an internal "design crit" series at [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor), establishing a recurring biweekly review process for all user-facing surfaces."
}
